channel.title: Kyunolab Mystery Archive
channel.description: Legends, folklore, mysteries, and strange tales.

item.title: Evil Eye Origin: How a Harmful Glance Became One of the World's Oldest Protective Beliefs
item.description: The evil eye is the belief that a harmful or envious glance can bring misfortune. Many cultures developed charms, gestures, or sayings to protect against it.

item.title: Sword in the Stone Legend: The Arthurian Object That Proved a King
item.description: The Sword in the Stone is an Arthurian legend in which the rightful king is revealed by pulling a sword from stone or anvil. The act proves legitimacy through an impossible test.

item.title: Mount Kailash Legendary Place: The Sacred Mountain People Circle But Do Not Climb
item.description: Mount Kailash is a sacred mountain in Tibet revered in several religious traditions. It is known for pilgrimage circuits around the mountain rather than ordinary summit climbing.

item.title: Raining Animals Phenomenon: When Fish, Frogs, and Weather Become Folklore
item.description: Raining animals refers to reports of fish, frogs, or other small creatures falling from the sky. Possible explanations often involve waterspouts or strong weather lifting animals from nearby water.

item.title: Lyonesse Lost Land Legend: The Drowned Kingdom at the Edge of Arthurian Memory
item.description: Lyonesse is a legendary lost land often placed off the coast of Cornwall. Later tradition connects it with drowned kingdoms, coastal memory, and Arthurian material.

item.title: Griffin Folklore: The Eagle-Lion Guardian of Treasure and Power
item.description: The griffin is a mythic creature usually combining the head and wings of an eagle with the body of a lion. It is often linked with guardianship, treasure, and powerful places.

item.title: Persephone and the Underworld: The Greek Myth Behind Loss, Return, and the Seasons
item.description: The Persephone myth tells how Persephone is taken to the underworld, how Demeter grieves, and how Persephone returns for part of the year. The cycle is often connected with seasonal change.

item.title: Dover Demon Legend: The Brief Massachusetts Sightings That Became a Modern Monster
item.description: The Dover Demon is a modern monster legend based on reports from Dover, Massachusetts, in 1977. Witnesses described a small, strange humanoid figure with an unusual head and glowing eyes.

item.title: Tomte and Nisse Folklore: The Small Household Guardian With a Sharp Temper
item.description: The tomte or nisse is a Nordic household spirit often linked with farms, barns, and family protection. It may help when treated well, but punish disrespect or neglect.

item.title: Amber Room Mystery: The Lost Treasure That Still Refuses a Final Ending
item.description: The Amber Room was an ornate chamber of amber panels and gold decoration that disappeared during World War II. A reconstruction exists, but the fate of the original remains disputed.

item.title: Eastern State Penitentiary Haunting: Why an Abandoned Prison Became a Strange Place
item.description: Eastern State Penitentiary is a former prison in Philadelphia whose architecture, solitary-confinement history, and abandoned appearance helped create a strong haunting reputation.

item.title: The Rake Creepypasta: How a Pale Figure Became Internet Folklore
item.description: The Rake is an internet horror figure usually described as a pale, unnatural humanoid creature. It spread through creepypasta-style posts, retellings, images, and fragmented witness-like accounts.

item.title: Resurrection Mary Legend: The Chicago Hitchhiker Who Keeps Returning to the Road
item.description: Resurrection Mary is a Chicago-area ghost legend about a young woman who appears near Archer Avenue, accepts or requests a ride, and vanishes near Resurrection Cemetery.

item.title: Horseshoe Luck Origin: Iron, Doorways, and How a Stable Object Became a Protective Sign
item.description: Horseshoe luck is a superstition that treats the horseshoe as a protective or lucky object, often placed above a doorway. Explanations usually involve iron, horses, blacksmithing, shape, and the boundary of the home.

item.title: Ark of the Covenant Legend: Sacred Chest, Lost Relic, and the Object No One Can Treat as Ordinary
item.description: The Ark of the Covenant is the sacred chest described in biblical tradition as connected with the tablets of the covenant and divine presence. Later legend focuses on its power, disappearance, and possible hidden location.

item.title: Stonehenge Legend: Ancient Stones, Merlin Stories, and the Monument That Still Refuses One Meaning
item.description: Stonehenge is a prehistoric stone monument in Wiltshire that has gathered archaeological interpretation, medieval legend, and modern sacred-place meanings. It is real, but no single explanation contains its full cultural afterlife.

item.title: Blood Rain Phenomenon: Red Rain, Omen Stories, and the Sky That Falls in the Wrong Color
item.description: Blood rain refers to rain that appears red or reddish because particles such as dust, sand, spores, or other material color the water. In many periods, red rain also became an omen story because the sky seemed to be raining blood.

item.title: Atlantis Lost City: Plato’s Island, Sunken Power, and the World That Vanished Beneath the Sea
item.description: Atlantis is the legendary island power described by Plato and later imagined as a lost civilization that sank beneath the sea. The original account appears in philosophical dialogue, not as a modern archaeological report.

item.title: Kraken Folklore: Sea Monster, Giant Arms, and the Creature Beneath the Ship
item.description: The Kraken is a northern sea-monster figure often imagined as an enormous creature beneath the surface, large enough to endanger ships. Later retellings frequently connect it with giant squid or octopus imagery.

item.title: Icarus Flight Myth: Wax Wings, the Sun, and the Warning About Flying Too High
item.description: Icarus is the Greek mythic figure who escapes Crete with wings made by Daedalus, but flies too close to the sun. The wax melts, the wings fail, and Icarus falls into the sea.
